claimType: illustrative-pro-forma — every number is hypothetical and framed as such in the script ("on a five hundred thousand dollar rental, with twenty-five percent down..."). No location-specific or live-market claims.

inputAssumptions.source: illustrative — round number for follow-along math
common minimum for non-owner-occupied investment loans (Fannie Mae / Freddie Mac investment-property guidelines, 2026)
illustrative current-tier rate for 30-yr fixed investment property; Freddie Mac PMMS national average for owner-occupied + typical investment spread of ~75-100 bps as of 2026-Q2
illustrative — typical for a $500K SFR rental in a moderately appreciating U.S. metro
illustrative pro-forma: P&I $2,495 + property tax $625 + insurance $80 = $3,200; the script's $2,800 is a round-number approximation. Computed P&I at 7%/30yr/$375K is $2,494.88.
illustrative: 5% vacancy + maintenance + capex reserves on $3,000 rent
derived: rent $3,000 − PITI $2,800 − opex $200 − reserves baked into PITI assumption ≈ thin margin; honest range is $0-$200

illustrative — long-run U.S. national-average single-family home price appreciation; FHFA House Price Index nominal annualized return ~3.5-4.5% over the past 30 years; Case-Shiller national ~3.5-4% nominal. Script frames as 'around three percent a year, the long-run national average' — defensible as a conservative round-number representation. NOT a current-market or location-specific claim.

illustrative: $500K purchase × 80% improvement / 20% land split. Common practice for SFR cost-segregation and standard depreciation schedules; varies by locality but 80% is a defensible starting assumption.
IRC §168(c) — Modified Accelerated Cost Recovery System (MACRS) residential rental property depreciation period. IRS Publication 527.
computed: $400,000 building basis ÷ 27.5 years = $14,545.45; rounded to $14,545. Script speaks 'fourteen thousand five hundred'.
IRS 2026 federal income tax brackets — 24% bracket covers $103,351-$197,300 single / $206,700-$394,600 married filing jointly. Illustrative typical-household bracket; not universal.
computed: $14,545 × 24% = $3,490.80; rounded to $3,491. Script speaks 'roughly thirty-five hundred'.

computedClaims.spokenInVO: fifteen thousand dollars in year one

computedClaims.formula: cumulative principal paid through month 12 on $375K @ 7% / 30yr standard amortization. Computed value $4,091; rounded to $3,800 for the script's round-number framing.
computedClaims.spokenInVO: about three thousand eight hundred dollars of principal disappears in year one
computedClaims.note: Script rounds DOWN slightly for narrative cleanness; rounded value is within 7% of computed and stays narrative-consistent.

hardFactCitations.source: IRC §1031 — like-kind exchange of real property held for investment or productive use in a trade or business
IRC §163; deductible as a business expense against rental income on Schedule E

hardFactCitations.claim: 24% federal tax bracket exists at the noted income range
hardFactCitations.source: Rev. Proc. 2025-XX (annual inflation adjustments); IRS 2026 tax brackets

hardFactCitations.claim: long-run U.S. national-average single-family appreciation around 3%
hardFactCitations.source: FHFA House Price Index (1991-2024 nominal annualized); S&P/Case-Shiller U.S. National Home Price Index (1990-2024). Both reside in the 3-4.5% nominal annualized range over long windows. 3% is a conservative-end round-number representation.

softClaimAudit.claim: It builds wealth four ways at once.
softClaimAudit.note: Aligns with widely accepted real-estate-investing pedagogy (BiggerPockets, Brandon Turner, et al).

softClaimAudit.claim: That's not luck. That's four things compounding at the same time.
softClaimAudit.note: Opinion stated as such; supported by the math shown on screen.

softClaimAudit.claim: Stacked together, they're the wealth most people can't see from the outside.
softClaimAudit.note: Outro framing; not a data claim.
